 Accounting I 
 
2 
 
   
 
1. Course description / Introduction to the course 
  
 
Accounting is the  language  of  business.  It  is an  essential  tool  for managing 
companies, offering 
➢ Information on the company’s progress 
➢ Support for decision-making 
➢ A means of communication across all areas of the company 
 
Financial Accounting  aims  to  systematically  collect  and  record  information  on 
everything  that  a  person  or  a  company  does  which  affects  their  financial  and 
economic  situation/wealth.  To  do  so,  there  are  a  set  of  concepts,  rules  and 
methods that have to be learned. 
 
In  the Financial  accounting  blocks  (Parts  I,  II  and  III) students  are  required  to 
record transactions  between  companies  and  third  parties,  allowing  firms  to 
prepare  quality  financial  statements  that  reflect  a  true  and fair  view  of  their 
situation.  In  contrast,  the  management  accounting  block (part  IV) focuses  on 
internal  information,  and  pays  special  attention  to  costing  techniques  that 
managers use to improve their information base for decision-making.   
 
Financial Accounting is essential for a finance career, including roles in banking, 
financial services, financial analysis, auditing, consulting, M&A, controlling, CFO 
responsibilities,  corporate  development,  strategic  planning,  treasury,  and  cash 
management. However, it is also vital for any business position, as it enables you 
to  understand  and  communicate  effectively  across  all  functional  areas  within a 
company. In  your current studies,  it's  important  to  grasp  all  accounting  and 
finance-related  courses,  such  as  Accounting  II,  Corporate  Finance,  Financial 
Strategy,   Management   Control,   Futures   and   Options,   and   Mergers   and 
Acquisitions. Finally,  on  a  personal  level,  it  equips  you  with  the  basics  to 
understand  daily  news,  make  informed  spending  decisions,  and  develop  skills 
like rigor and analytical thinking. 
 
 
 
 
ne 2015 
  
Accounting is the language of business  

 Accounting I 
 
3 
 
   
 
2. Learning objectives and competencies 
  
 
At the end of the course students are able to :  
- Evaluate and understand the usefulness of accounting for other areas of 
a company as well as externally 
- Understand how  accounting  works and  know  the basic  principles and 
criteria it is based on. 
- Understand the meaning of standalone financial statements and how they 
are affected by the accounting events that occur in a company.  
- Prepare financial statements based on current regulations. 
- Identify   different   cost   behaviours.   Prepare   and   analyse   information 
included in different costing systems. Prepare stock valuation information. 
Understand and apply cost-volume-profit analysys techniques 
 
 
The course will contribute to develop these competences: 
1- Knowledge acquisition, comprehension and structuring 
2- Application of knowledge to achieve results 
3- Taking decisions/making judgments 
4-Understand    management    discretion    and    choose    between    different 
management options and techniques 
 
 
 
 
  

 Accounting I 
 
4 
 
   
 
3. Course format and methodological approach 
  
To achieve the objectives of the course all sessions will have two parts:  
- Concepts: lectures,   class   notes, and   class   materials   devoted   to 
presentation  of  frameworks  and  concepts  that  are  useful  for  accounting 
practice.  
- Practice: discuss class activities and clarify questions about concepts and 
their practical application.   
You will find a Planning for each session with the activities to be done BEFORE 
CLASS, IN CLASS or AFTER CLASS. It is important to do all activities on time in 
order to maximize the value added of each session. Tutorials of small group or 
individual tutorial sessions will help you solve questions you might have.  
➢ AI positioning for learning activities 
Generative  AI  tools may  be  used  in  this  course  for  assignments  or  other 
coursework only when explicitly permitted by the instructor. While thoughtful use 
of these AI tools can enhance the learning experience, it is important to recognize 
that such tools sometimes produce fabricated, inaccurate, or false material. As a 
student,  you  are  responsible  for  thoroughly  reviewing  any  AI-generated  or  AI-
corrected content for accuracy, ensuring that your final submissions reflect your 
own  work  and  critical  thinking.  All  use  of  AI  must  be  documented  with  proper 
citations. Any inappropriate or unauthorized use of AI tools will be considered a 
serious academic integrity violation, subject to the policies of ESADE Business 
School. 
All students are expected to show:  
✓ Punctuality 
✓ Respect 
✓ Active contribution 
✓ Use of laptops, Iphones & others only for academic purposes during the 
class 
 
 
 
 

 Accounting I 
 
5

 Accounting I 
 
6 
 
   
4. Course Contents:  
  
Basic Concepts 
Introduction to the accounting function  
Regulation/standards 
Financial Statements 
Accounting principles  
Balance sheet: Assets and liabilities. Net equity 
Accounting Fundamental Equation 
Income Statement: Income/expenses/profit 
 
Full accounting procedure 
Accounting Equivalences 
Accounts. Groups. 
Creating financial statements: Books.  
Full accounting procedure 
 
 
Current assets/liabilities (non-financial assets) 
Accrual accounting: concept and application to calculate net income 
Inventory  purchases/sales  and changes  in  inventories. Operating  margin  and 
Cost  of  Goods  Sold. Value  Added  Tax. Valuation  standards and  impairments. 
Debtors and creditors. Operating income and expenses. Income tax. 
 
 
Non-current assets/Financial liabilities/Equity 
Tangible fixed assets: Purchases, sales, impairments and depreciation. Valuation 
standards.  
Intangible  assets: Purchases,  sales,  impairments and  amortization.  Valuation 
standards.  
Financial liabilities: options and impact on financial statements 
Equity: composition and meaning 
 
 
Management Accounting 
Cost classification and behaviour. Cost-volume-profit analyses 
Full costing and inventory valuation 
 
  
PART I: Financial Accounting 
PART II: Financial Accounting 
PART III: Financial Accounting 
PART IV: Management Accounting 
WRAP-UP 

 Accounting I 
 
7 
 
   
 
To pass the course, the minimum grade is 5/10. The assessment breakdown 
in the first and second call is the same: In case of third and fourth calls, the course 
grade is the exam grade. 
 
20% 
Tests: 5 online tests during the course. You will be informed about 
the dates for the tests. Physical attendance is mandatory. Missing one 
test is a 0. At the end of the course, the lowest grade is eliminated. 
10% 
Class   participation: Grading   class   participation   is   necessarily 
subjective.  It  refers  to  behavior  (*),  contribution and attendance. 
Some of the criteria for evaluating effective class participation include: 
Has the student paid attention to the faculty or other students during 
the whole session? Do comments show an understanding of theories 
and  concepts  presented  in  class  lectures?  Has  the  student  worked 
on the assignments and activities?  
Attendance, which will be taken via QR codes at the beginning of the 
class.  Any  technical  difficulties  should  be  reported in  class;  no 
modification   will   be   made   after   class   (e.g.,   student   emails). 
Remember that Esade’s regulations require 80% attendance to be 
eligible for final grading. Late attendance penalizes the participation 
grade. 
Partcipation: 
9-10 Consistent and Distinctive Contributions 
6-8 Appropriate Interventions 
1-5 Mere Attendance, Peripheral Participation  
0 Discourteous behavior 
(*)  Classroom behavior  that  interferes  with  either  the  instructor’s 
ability to conduct the class or the ability of students to benefit from the 
instruction   is   not   acceptable.   Students   engaging   in   improper 
classroom behavior may have points deducted from their total points 
in the course. Any serious offence may cause the loss of one call. 
20% 
Group project. Unjustified absences and at free riding attitudes will 
be  penalized. 
50% 
Final  exam (minimum  grade  requirement): To  pass  the  course, 
students  need  to  obtain  a minimum of  4/10  in  the  final  exam. (The 
overall course passing grade is a 5/10.)

Third and fourth calls.Special situations: 
Students repeating the first year course who have the subject Basics 
of  Financial  Accounting  pending,  will  only  be  examined on  the  part 
corresponding  to Basics  of  Financial  Accounting..  The Advanced 
Financial   Accounting  grade   will   be   taken   into   account   when 
calculating the final grade for Accounting I. 
Students  repeating  the first  year course  who  have  the  subject 
Advanced  Financial  Accounting  pending,  will  only  be  examined  on 
the part corresponding to Advanced Financial Accounting, The Basics 
of  Financial  Accounting grade  will  be  taken  into  account  when 
calculating the final grade for Accounting I. 
 
This  course  follows  strictly  ESADE’s  Honour  Code  and  thus,  all 
students  are  expected  to  behave  according  to  the  principle  of 
academic  honesty.  Conducting  any  irregularity  or  violation  of  the 
ESADE’s  Honour  Code  will  imply  the  application  of  the  penalties 
detailed in the Internal Rules and Regulations. 
Any  unpermitted evidence of  misconduct will  be  treated 
according    to    the current Internal    Program    Normative 
(Reglamento de Régimen Interior) 
According  to  the  Program  Normative,  a minimum  of  80%  of 
class attendance is required to do the Final Exam in the first 
call,  and  of  50%  in  the  second  call. Any  justified  absence 
should be discussed with Program Direction.
